dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a priority 1 call. Male patient fallen in parking lot.
Hi, I'm Jordan. I'm an EMT. Can you explain what happened to the patient here?
He fell and hit his head on a concrete block. He's been out for maybe five minutes.
Did he tell you anything before he hit his head, like he was injured elsewhere or in pain?
No. He wasn't speaking much. He was acting confused before the fall, then he just collapsed.
Eyes open to pain. Verbal-only grunts. Motor-movement without purpose. GCS is 11.
He doesn't seem to recognize people. Wasn't alert when we initially arrived.
Pupils are pinpoint bilaterally. Eyes respond to verbal and painful stimuli only.
He utters words randomly. Doesn't form coherent phrases.
Moves when stimulated but without direction. Motor GCS suggests impairment.
Patient's head is positioned forward-no immediate airway blockage seen.
Respirations are shallow at 12 a minute. Chest movement equal at first glance.
Breaths are consistent. No flaring, use of accessory muscles.
Pulse is strong and regular. Skin warm and dry, no paleness seen so far.
Heart rate is 100. Patient is tachycardic, but rhythm regular.
12 breaths per minute. Regular rhythm.
Blood pressure is 133/89. Vital appears stable at the moment.
SpO2 reading is 90.
Blood sugar is 119. Not in hypoglycemic range.
12-lead just captured-interpretation shows sinus rhythm and minimal LVH in V5.
No visible skin trauma, hives, rashes, or signs of bites or stings.
Head has no dent, deformity, or lacerations. Palpation reveals no step-offs or tenderness.
Trachea is midline. No evidence of jugular distension or Battle signs.
No drainage from ears or nose. Oral cavity visible but patient unable to cooperate fully.
Lung sounds are clear bilateral-no wheezes or crackles.
Soft, non-tender abdomen with normal bowel sounds.
No visible discharges or urinary stains. Bowel or bladder parameter unremarkable.
All limbs are intact, no angulation. Strength and sensation not assessable due to patient status.
He has a medical history. Do you know anything about his conditions?
He has CHF, hypertension, liver disease, and AFib.
Allergic to anything specific? How about medications he regularly takes?
He's allergic to fish. Takes Eliquis, Lasix, Cordarone, and a multivitamin.
Did he say anything about pain or aches before this fall occurred?
No. He was dazed before but didn't mention injury.
No subjective pain reported. Patient is altered and non-verbal at this time.
No visible signs of discomfort. Unable to elicit pain response due to patient status.
Cervical collar applied over shoulders to stabilize c-spine.
Full spine immobilization with backboard and spider straps initiated.
IV access established in left wrist-18-gauge cannula placed.
Cardiac monitoring started with 12-lead EKG for arrhythmic surveillance.
Smell of ETOH on breath-patient's history of alcohol use noted.
Repeating EKG shows unchanged rhythm-still sinus with minimal LVH pattern.
Blood pressure is lower-108/69 now. No shock signs but trending downward.
Pulse has jumped to 178. Tachycardia noted.
O2 saturation dropped to zero. Breathing no longer detectable by visual cues.
Glucose monitor displayed no reading-device may not be fully functional.
Worsening vitals: P 178, BP 108/69, SpO2 0. Patient is apneic now.
Known medications remain the same-Eliquis, Lasix, Cordarone, and vitamins.
Altered mental status, fall with head contact, and recent tachycardia-field impression supports trauma protocol.
Using protocol 4-1 for general trauma management with head involvement.
Transport ordered to VCU Health Cold-prioritized with lights and sirens.
Spine immobilized using backboard and manual lift-Richmond Fire assisted.
IV fluids and EKG monitoring confirmed running and stable for transport.
ETOH noted on patient's breath-from provider's assessment, no drugs observed or reported.
Can he walk again if it is a spine injury? Is his brain affected?
Depends on injuries discovered in the trauma bay. Hope remains with full assessment.
Handoff for 51-year-old male with suspected head trauma and altered mental status.
Comorbidities include CHF, AFib, hypertension, liver disease, and fish allergy.
12-lead EKG and IV fluids in place. Vital signs reported and pending further evaluation.
No known drug use-only observation is ethanol on breath, according to provider notes.
No signs of petechiae or purpura-appears normal given patient's ongoing AMS.
No lymphadenopathy, neck masses, or swallowing difficulties observed.
No foreign bodies or blockage in nasal or oral passages.
No cardiac murmurs or S3/S4 gallops in auscultation.
No palpable masses or rebound tenderness-reevaluated during full trauma workup.
No rectal bleeding or discoloration in urine-patient unable to describe voiding habits.
Patient does not use limbs-no history of any pre-event weakness reported.
Do you know when he last had food or drink?
I can't say. He looked confused the whole time.
What was going on in the minutes before the fall?
The caller said he hit his head and lost consciousness.
No medications administered on scene per protocol given patient's GCS and status.
Full spine immobilization completed via manual transfer and backboard.
Patient has not reported pain at any point during initial or repeated assessments.
GCS is ultimately 13 (eye=3, verbal=4, motor=6) as documented in the report.
ETOH observed-patient has a history of alcohol dependence per medical history.
Spinal precautions maintained during the entire transport phase.
Redocumenting medications-no changes-still Eliquis, Lasix, and Cordarone.
IV started on first attempt-needle placed in left wrist.
IV line open and patent-prepared for fluids or meds if needed during transport.
No pain response even when assessed for again. Unable to confirm using O.P.Q.R.S.T.
No visible signs of instability during stretcher transitions or ambulance movement.
Decisions during transfer based on protocol-no alcohol intervention requested.
Memory assessment was not valid-patient unable to access memory per evaluator.
No bites, stings, rashes, or abnormal skin texture-AMS may still delay skin assessment.
Are there prior falls or trauma history relevant?
I can't say. He was sitting on a low wall with his head on concrete.
Nuchal rigidity absent-flexibility preserved during exam.
BP remains at 108/69. No hint of pulmonary or cardiac compromise to date.
Spine status maintained via backboard through full transport.
Patient was apneic, but no signs of cardiac arrest prior to hospital transfer.
Spinal care continued-with no movement from point of assessment to ED.
No additional fluid boluses-monitoring instead per ongoing intervals.
Time-sensitive injuries-reason for primary destination at VCU Health Cold.
Patient posture considered most neutral for comfort and care.
Noted per protocol-keeping patient supine and secured at all times.
Followed spinal protocol-no movement or rotation of patient during securing.
Heard cardiac tones, but no murmur, rubs, or gallops-still sinus rhythm.
No new pain development assessed-even when examined more recently.
Final recheck-no signs of deterioration prior to hospital arrival.
Transporting in monitor-a continuous check of ventricular function.
Trauma protocol followed with monitoring and intervention as dictated per protocol 4-1.
No changes after reassessments-senior provider confirmed care was sufficient.
Patient placed in bed with minimal spinal disruption via 4-person bed narrow.
No new interventions since handover-focus remained on transport and readying for ED.
IV drug compatible per transport-nothing declined or deemphasized in scope of care.
Known alcohol dependence-documented critically but not as initiating event.
Initial observation was unremarkable-follow-up notes no issues post transport.
Transfer of care complete with RN: patient alertness was finally determined after arrival.
Fully transferred via stretcher with backboard intact per ED protocol.
Hospital en route with full vitals, care summary, and drug list in record.
Direct handoff expected-hospital team aware of trauma and GCS level after initial care.
No alcohol withdrawal signs-patient remains partially unresponsive throughout.
Final log completed on time-transport documentation reviewed and approved.
